Roe deer abundance

[NO_RODA_001]

Author
Affiliation

Aud H. Halbritter

University of Bergen

Published

May 15, 2026

Warning: There was 1 warning in `dplyr::mutate()`.
ℹ In argument: `Variable = dplyr::case_match(...)`.
Caused by warning:
! `case_match()` was deprecated in dplyr 1.2.0.
ℹ Please use `recode_values()` instead.

Recommended citation: Halbritter, A. H. 2026. Roe deer abundance [Rådyrbestand] (ID(s): NO_RODA_001) v. 1.0.0. ecRxiv: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_RODA_001

TipMetadata
TipLog
  • May 15, 2026 - Initial draft for ecRxiv

1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale: The roe deer (Capreolus capreolus) is a cervid associated with forest ecosystems in Norway. In the period with reasonably good overview of cervid densities in Norway (about the last 200 years), populations have been strongly influenced by human activity, and in several areas current roe deer densities are considered higher than the reference condition.

  2. Methods: The roe deer abundance indicator (rådyrbestand) expresses population density as number of individuals per km² below the climatic tree line, reported at county (fylke) level, as described on Naturindeksen – rådyr. Underlying population information for roe deer in Norway is linked to the national monitoring programme for cervids (elg, hjort, rådyr, and villrein), which has operated since 1991 and is owned by Landbruksdirektoratet and run by NINA (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026). Much of the monitoring material is collected from animals harvested during hunting (for example lower jaw and slaughter weight) (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026). The reference condition is an expert-assessed state in which roe deer may occur in suitable areas under current climate and predator regimes, with hunting management that keeps densities from rising far above the reference optimum; impacts on other biodiversity are considered less severe for this species than for larger cervids. For Naturindeks, estimated abundance or equivalent indices by county are normalised using a unimodal scaling function. Scaled county values are aggregated to five geographic regions and national level.

  3. Interpretation: An indicator value of 1 means that roe deer abundance in the reporting unit is at least as large as expected under the reference condition used in Naturindeksen. Values decreasing toward zero indicate that the population index is further below that reference.

  4. Results and development needs:

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

  1. Bakgrunn og begrunnelse: Rådyr (Capreolus capreolus) er et hjortedyr knyttet til skogøkosystemer i Norge. I perioden med rimelig oversikt over hjortevilttettheter i Norge (ca. 200 år) har bestandene vært sterkt påvirket av menneskelig aktivitet, og i flere områder vurderes dagens rådyrtettheter som høyere enn referansetilstanden. Data leveres på fylkesnivå.

  2. Metoder: Indikatoren for rådyrbestand uttrykker tetthet som antall individer per km² under den klimatiske tregrensen, rapportert på fylkesnivå, som beskrevet på Naturindeksen – rådyr. Underliggende bestandsinformasjon er knyttet til det nasjonale overvåkingsprogrammet for hjortevilt (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026), med mye materiale fra jakt (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026) og statistikk via Hjorteviltregisteret (Landbruksdirektoratet 2026). Referansetilstanden er en ekspertvurdert tilstand der rådyr kan forekomme i egnete områder under dagens klima og rovdyrregimer, med jaktforvaltning som holder tettheten fra å stige langt over referanseoptimum; påvirkning på annet biologisk mangfold vurderes som mindre alvorlig for denne arten enn for større hjortedyr. For Naturindeks normaliseres fylkesverdier med en unimodals skaleringsfunksjon. Skalerte fylkesverdier aggregeres til fem geografiske regioner og nasjonalt nivå.

  3. Tolkning: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at rådyrbestanden i rapporteringsenheten er minst like stor som forventet under referansetilstanden. Verdier mot null viser at bestandsindekset ligger lenger under referansen.

  4. Resultater og utviklingsbehov:

2. About the underlying data

The roe deer abundance data comes from the national cervid monitoring programme and Hjorteviltregisteret (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026; Landbruksdirektoratet 2026).

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.

To produce indicator estimates for these years, input is typically combined over a short window (typically two to three years), as specified in the Naturindeks indicator protocol.

Spatially, data and indicator values are handled at county (fylke) scale as IC units in Naturindeksen, then aggregated to five geographic regions and national level for reporting.

2.2 Sampling design

The national monitoring programme for cervids collects most material from harvested animals during hunting seasons (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026). For roe deer, samples can include lower jaw and slaughter weight (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026). Further methodological detail for how county abundance enters Naturindeks is given on Naturindeksen – rådyr.

2.3 Original units

The roe deer abundance is expressed as number of individuals per km².

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

Monitoring programme: (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026) and Hjorteviltregisteret (Landbruksdirektoratet 2026).

Methodological documentation: Naturindeksen – rådyr.

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

B3 Functional state characteristics.

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

The indicator may covary with other cervid abundance indicators (moose, red deer) and with other species indicators that share similar habitats or data sources.

3.4 Impact factors

3.5 Spatial aggregation

Following the metadata pathway Aggregate (population model, county level) – [Scale – Truncate] – Aggregate (national level): population estimates or indices are available per county (IC unit in Naturindeksen). Each county value is normalised with a unimodal scaling function (optimum at X100; lower and upper reference values at X0; see Section 4.2), not with simple linear scaling between a single minimum and maximum only. Regional (east, south, west, central, north) and national indicator values are then obtained as area-weighted combinations of the scaled county values.

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition for roe deer is…

4.2 Reference levels

For this indicator, normalisation uses a unimodal scaling function rather than linear scaling. Optimum abundance corresponds to X100. The lower and upper reference values on the variable scale are both set at X0, so that densities both below and above the optimum are scaled towards worst condition. County-specific values for these reference levels are defined in Naturindeksen; see Naturindeksen – rådyr and the imported referenceValues object once data are available in Section 7.

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

Reference levels and indicator values apply at county (fylke) scale (IC units). Aggregated results for the five Naturindeks geographic regions and for Norway are summaries of those units with area weights. Normalisation should be done at county scale before regional or national aggregation.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

Reference levels are held fixed in this workflow (their own uncertainties are not propagated). Regional and national values are area-weighted summaries of counties, so they inherit both model uncertainty at county scale and any mismatch between county boundaries and ecological variation within regions.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

The intervals in Table 2 and Figure 2 are inferential: they describe uncertainty around the estimated area-weighted mean indicator for each reporting unit (a geographic region or Norway as a whole), given the statistical models used for county-level abundance in Naturindeksen.

For each county and assessment year, Naturindeks provides a predictive distribution for abundance (expressed on the variable scale before scaling). We draw many random values from that distribution per county, scale each draw to the 0–1 indicator scale using the county reference optimum and unimodal scaling, then build a large set of possible county indicators. To obtain uncertainty for a region, we repeat the following 1000 times: draw one random county indicator per county in that region, take the forest-area-weighted average across those counties, and record that regional mean. The median of those regional means is reported as the point estimate; the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles form an approximate 95% interval, and the 25% and 75% quantiles form an approximate interquartile range. For Norway, the same idea is applied one level up: in each replicate we combine the five regional means from that same replicate using weights proportional to total forest area in each region, then summarise the resulting national means in the same way. This approach reflects uncertainty in the county models and in the scaled values, while treating the regional or national figure as a single summary parameter rather than as a random draw from one county.

6. References

Landbruksdirektoratet. 2026. “Hjorteviltregisteret.” https://www.hjorteviltregisteret.no.
Landbruksdirektoratet, and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research. 2026. “Nasjonalt Overvåkingsprogram for Hjortevilt.” https://hjortevilt.no/forskning-og-viltovervaking/nasjonalt-bestandsovervakningsprogram-for-hjortevilt-bop.
Norwegian Institute for Nature Research, and partners. 2025. “Norwegian Nature Index (Naturindeks).” https://www.naturindeks.no.

7. Import and prepare datasets

Run the download chunk once to refresh the local export. This project’s renv environment installs NIcalc from GitHub ChloeRN/NIcalc, branch include_ReadOnly_access (see renv.lock and install_deps.R); that build defaults to the Naturindeks read-only API user, so you do not need username / password arguments here. When NINAnor/NIcalc PR #59 is merged into NINAnor/NIcalc, you can switch the renv install back to the main repo and update the lockfile. We save the RDS export under data/ so that we do not need to download the data every time we run the analysis.

Download roe deer data from Naturindeks API 
rodaImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Rådyr"),
  year = c("1990", "2000", "2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)

saveRDS(rodaImport, here::here("data/rodaImport.rds"))

8. Spatial units

According to the Naturindeks terminology, the Norwegian counties are BSunits, the 15 counties are IC units, and the five geographic regions in Norway (east, south, west, central, north) are NI units. We can think of the counties as ecosystem assets, and mainland Norway as the Ecosystem accounting Area.

9. Analyses

This section uses the following workflow: we take variable values and reference levels from the Naturindeks import, normalise at county scale, then propagate uncertainty and aggregate spatially with area weights. At regional and national scale, uncertainties are inferential: each replicate is an area-weighted mean based on one draw per county (then per region), not a random draw from a pooled mixture of counties. We use unimodal scaling and forest area (Skog) as weights.

County-level abundance in the database is stored as a predictive distribution (parameters or custom distributions), not only as a point estimate. Reference optimum density per county comes from the reference slice of the same download (expectedValue for the reference year). We ignore uncertainty in reference levels.

Assemble import and indicator object

assembleNiObject() reshapes rodaImport so that indicator observations are in one list element per assessment year, and so that municipality-level BSunits (with forest area and NI region code part) are aligned with the thematic index structure used in Naturindeks.

Assemble roe deer NI object (thematic, predefined regions) 
rodaAssemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = rodaImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)

Check input data ..... 

Elicitate incomplete reference values and indicator observations ..... 

Assemble dataset .....

Reference levels (X100)

Before scaling, we need the county-specific optimum on the variable scale (roe deer density, ind. km⁻² below the tree line). These are read from referenceValues in the RDS export (rows with yearId == 0 or reference year name), one x100 per IC unit (county).

County reference optimum (X100) from downloaded Naturindeks data 
x100 <- rodaImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  dplyr::filter(.data$yearId == 0L | grepl("Referanse", .data$yearName, fixed = FALSE)) |>
  dplyr::distinct(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue)
x100 |>
  gt::gt()
ICunitId ICunitName x100
3646 Østfold 1e+00
3647 Akershus 1e+00
3648 Hedmark 1e-01
3649 Oppland 1e-01
3650 Buskerud 1e-01
3651 Vestfold 1e+00
3652 Telemark 5e-01
3653 Aust-Agder 5e-01
3654 Vest-Agder 5e-01
3655 Rogaland 5e-01
3656 Hordaland 1e+00
3657 Sogn og Fjordane 1e+00
3658 Møre og Romsdal 1e+00
3659 Sør-Trøndelag 3e-01
3660 Nord-Trøndelag 3e-01
3661 Nordland 1e-01
14180 Troms 1e-04

Resample non-scaled county-scale variables

For each assessment year, Naturindeks stores county values as a parameterised distribution (or a custom distribution). We draw nsim = 1000 random values per county with NIcalc::sampleObsMat(). Rows with a standard distribution family use observation type tradObs; rows with only a custom distribution use customObs.

Assessment years are the following: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024. The result dat is a long table of simulated variable values (value) by ICunitId and year (not yet on the 0–1 indicator scale).

Observation types for sampleObsMat 
myYears <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- vector("list", length(myYears))
for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  obs <- rodaAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName
  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"
  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}
Resample county-scale variables (non-scaled) 
dat <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = rodaAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = rodaAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = rodaAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = rodaAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = rodaAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = rodaAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  colnames(myMat) <- paste0("s", seq_len(ncol(myMat)))
  myMat <- myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId"), names_to = "sim_col", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(
      sim_id = readr::parse_number(.data$sim_col),
      year = myYears[i]
    ) |>
    select(ICunitId, sim_id, value, year)

  dat <- dat |>
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(dat) |>
  gt::gt()
ICunitId sim_id value year
14180 1 0.0016465707 1990
14180 2 0.0009391679 1990
14180 3 0.0033007675 1990
14180 4 0.0024809164 1990
14180 5 0.0014899922 1990
14180 6 0.0011978527 1990

Scaling to indicator values (unimodal)

Normalisation is done at county scale before any regional or national aggregation. Each draw is joined to its county x100, then mapped to [0, 1] with ecTools::ec_normalise() in two-sided unimodal mode: x0 = 0 and x100 = optimum from the download define the best condition; x0h = 2 * x100 is a simple upper anchor for densities above the optimum. We use the default linear transformation (fun = "linear"), without an additional sigmoid step (Norwegian Institute for Nature Research and partners 2025).

Join X100 and unimodal scaling to indicator scale 
dat <- dat |>
  left_join(x100 |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)), by = join_by(ICunitId))

ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = dat$value,
  x0 = 0,
  x100 = dat$x100,
  x0h = 2 * dat$x100,
  fun = "linear"
)

dat <- dat |> add_column(indicator = ind)

The scatter plot below (Figure 1) shows the mapping from variable to indicator for each county (one facet per ICunitName). The figure is saved when the document is rendered.

Save normalisation diagnostic figure (English) 
roe_deer_plot <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ dat$ICunitName)
ggsave(filename = here::here("img/roda_norm_plot_eng.png"), plot = roe_deer_plot, scale = 1.5)
Saving 10.5 x 7.5 in image
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/roda_norm_plot_eng.png"))

Aggregation weights (forest area by county)

Spatial aggregation uses area below the tree line recorded in Naturindeks (Skog on each municipality in BSunits). Municipalities are linked to counties via ICunits, then forest area is summed to county (ICunitId) and the NI region part (N, C, W, S, E) is kept for the next step. Each row in dat then carries forest_area and part for weighting.

Forest area by county and NI region for aggregation weights 
aux <- as_tibble(rodaAssemble$BSunits) |>
  rename(BSunitId = id)

IC_BC_link <- rodaImport$ICunits |>
  select(ICunitId = id, BSunitId)

aux <- aux |>
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId))

aux <- aux |>
  group_by(ICunitId) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(Skog), part = unique(part)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

dat <- dat |>
  left_join(aux, by = join_by(ICunitId))

Aggregate to geographic regions (inferential uncertainty)

For each Monte Carlo replicate (b = 1, , 1000) and each region–year, we draw one posterior sample per county (from that county’s 1000 simulations in dat), then compute the forest-area-weighted mean across counties in the region. The resulting 1000 values describe uncertainty around the regional mean indicator.

Bootstrap distribution of area-weighted regional means 
regions <- c("N", "C", "W", "S", "E")
B <- 1000L

out <- tibble(id = seq_len(B))

for (i in regions) {
  for (n in myYears) {
    d_yr <- dat |>
      filter(.data$part == i, .data$year == n)

    counties <- sort(unique(d_yr$ICunitId))
    w <- aux |>
      filter(.data$ICunitId %in% counties) |>
      arrange(match(.data$ICunitId, counties))

    by_county <- split(d_yr$indicator, d_yr$ICunitId)[counties]

    col_vals <- vapply(
      seq_len(B),
      function(b) {
        draws <- vapply(by_county, sample, numeric(1), size = 1L)
        stats::weighted.mean(draws, w$forest_area)
      },
      numeric(1)
    )

    out <- out |>
      add_column(!!paste(i, n, sep = "_") := col_vals)
  }
}
head(out)
# A tibble: 6 × 31
     id  N_1990 N_2000 N_2010 N_2014 N_2019 N_2024 C_1990 C_2000 C_2010 C_2014
  <int>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1     1 0.0247  0.0726  0.521 0.0450  0      0      0.225  0.578 0.557   0.181
2     2 0.0513  0.0488  0.479 0       0      0.420  0.377  0.101 0.229   0.104
3     3 0.0732  0.131   0.348 0.374   0.156  0      0.444  0.258 0.0869  0.597
4     4 0.0221  0.108   0.348 0       0.417  0      0.552  0.409 0.0926  0.203
5     5 0.00784 0.0458  0.476 0.438   0      0.142  0.538  0.786 0.314   0.410
6     6 0.0214  0.210   0.188 0.226   0      0      0.814  0.331 0.136   0.460
# ℹ 20 more variables: C_2019 <dbl>, C_2024 <dbl>, W_1990 <dbl>, W_2000 <dbl>,
#   W_2010 <dbl>, W_2014 <dbl>, W_2019 <dbl>, W_2024 <dbl>, S_1990 <dbl>,
#   S_2000 <dbl>, S_2010 <dbl>, S_2014 <dbl>, S_2019 <dbl>, S_2024 <dbl>,
#   E_1990 <dbl>, E_2000 <dbl>, E_2010 <dbl>, E_2014 <dbl>, E_2019 <dbl>,
#   E_2024 <dbl>

Aggregate to national level (inferential uncertainty)

For each year and replicate (b), the national value is the forest-area-weighted mean of the five regional values from that same replicate (aux2 gives total forest area per region). This propagates uncertainty to the national mean in the same way as at regional scale.

Bootstrap distribution of area-weighted national means 
aux2 <- aux |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(.data$forest_area), .groups = "drop")

w_region <- aux2$forest_area[match(regions, aux2$part)]

out2 <- tibble(id = seq_len(B))

for (n in myYears) {
  nat_vals <- vapply(
    seq_len(B),
    function(b) {
      reg_draws <- vapply(
        regions,
        function(r) out[[paste(r, n, sep = "_")]][b],
        numeric(1)
      )
      stats::weighted.mean(reg_draws, w_region)
    },
    numeric(1)
  )

  out2 <- out2 |>
    add_column(!!paste("All", n, sep = "_") := nat_vals)
}

head(out2)
# A tibble: 6 × 7
     id All_1990 All_2000 All_2010 All_2014 All_2019 All_2024
  <int>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1     1    0.189    0.153    0.295    0.243   0.270    0.183 
2     2    0.152    0.108    0.266    0.108   0.213    0.171 
3     3    0.164    0.172    0.131    0.320   0.146    0.0519
4     4    0.319    0.217    0.244    0.151   0.151    0.251 
5     5    0.282    0.187    0.321    0.230   0.129    0.178 
6     6    0.231    0.183    0.263    0.180   0.0660   0.150
Bootstrap distribution of area-weighted national means 
out <- out |>
  bind_cols(out2 |> select(-id))

10. Results

From the 1000 bootstrap replicates per region and year in out, we report the median as the point estimate and use the 0.25% and 99.75% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval for the area-weighted mean indicator (inferential uncertainty, not descriptive between-county spread). The table (Table 2) and time series (Figure 2) summarise regional and national (All) values.

Code 
summary_out <- out |>
  pivot_longer(cols = !any_of("id")) |>
  separate_wider_delim(cols = name, delim = "_", names = c("part", "year")) |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(value), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.0025), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.25), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.75), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.975), 2),
  ) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B3",
    Indicator = "Roe deer abundance",
    Ecosystem = "Boreal/montane forests",
    ID = "NO_RODA_001"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    part = case_when(
      part == "All" ~ "Norway",
      part == "N" ~ "North",
      part == "C" ~ "Central",
      part == "W" ~ "West",
      part == "S" ~ "South",
      part == "E" ~ "East",
      TRUE ~ part
    )
  )
Code 
summary_out |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part, group = part)) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975),
    size = 2,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)
  ) +
  geom_line(position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "Year") +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", name = "Region")
Code 
summary_out |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()

11. Export file

Export resampled indicator draws 
write.csv(out, here::here("data/results_NO_RODA_001_v1.0.0.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

12. Author contributions

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on Naturindeks (Norwegian Institute for Nature Research and partners 2025).

TipSession Info

This information is mainly aimed at developers that need to recreate the R environment that produced the html. Important note: this information is about the R session that was running when the html was rendered, and if some of the code chunks were not evaluated when the html was rendered, they may have been executed in a different environment.
